speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back to Git Going with GitHub. This is episode 33: Publishing with GitHub Pages. I am Alex, and today we are turning Publishing with GitHub Pages from a list of instructions into a working mental model.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And I am Jamie. I will stop us whenever the instructions sound simple on paper but might feel different with a keyboard and screen reader.

speaker: ALEX
text: Free static site hosting, custom domains, HTTPS, and accessibility. That is the surface description. Underneath it, we are building judgment: where to focus, what to ignore, and how to verify the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So we are not using the audio as a shortcut around learning. We are using it to make the learning easier to enter.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. A good audio lesson gives someone enough context to try the work with confidence, even before they open the written material.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay, set the room for us. What are we walking into?

speaker: ALEX
text: How to Deploy a Static Website Directly from Your Repository: GitHub Pages lets you publish a static website straight from a GitHub repository - no server, no hosting bill, no deployment pipeline required for simple sites. The next useful detail is concrete: This appendix explains how to enable it, what it can publish, and how to ensure the published site meets the same accessibility standards as your source code.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next layer is this. Learning Cards: GitHub Pages Overview has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here are the anchors worth keeping. The Pages settings are under Settings (gear icon in repository navigation) then "Pages" in the left sidebar under the "Code and automation" group heading. Branch and folder selectors in the Pages settings are standard select elements -- navigate with arrow keys to choose your publishing source. After deployment, the published URL appears as a link at the top of the Pages settings page. GitHub Pages settings use the same layout as other repository settings -- look for the "Pages" link in the left sidebar after clicking Settings. The deployment status indicator shows a green checkmark for successful deployments and a red X for failures. Published sites inherit no special styling from GitHub -- ensure your site's CSS provides adequate contrast and font sizing.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the one idea that makes the next few steps less mysterious?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside 1. What GitHub Pages Is: GitHub Pages is a static site hosting service built into GitHub. That matters in practice: It serves files directly from a branch or folder in your repository at a URL of the form.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That feels much more doable when you say it as one move.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The magic is not speed. The magic is knowing what changed and why it matters.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now bring the learner back to the room. What it is good for has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: For a learner, the useful signals are concrete. Documentation sites. Workshop materials (like this project). Project landing pages. Personal portfolios. Simple blogs via Jekyll.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the ordered workflow?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: GitHub will build and deploy within a minute or two. The listener should be able to check this: The URL appears at the top of the Pages settings once the first deployment succeeds.

speaker: ALEX
text: The parts worth keeping in working memory are these. Deploy from a branch - serve files directly from a branch/folder. GitHub Actions - use a workflow to build and deploy. Select the branch (e.g. main or master). Select the folder: / (root) or /docs.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is go to the repository on GitHub.com. Step two is click Settings (the gear icon in the top navigation). Step three is in the left sidebar, scroll to Code and automation and click Pages. Step four is under Build and deployment, choose your publishing source. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the sequence, because order matters here.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, if using "Deploy from a branch". Then, click Save. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful habit is simple: orient, act, verify, then continue. That pause between action and trust is part of the work.

speaker: ALEX
text: That matters because of the next idea. Screen reader navigation for the Pages settings page has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: On the ground, that means a few things. The Settings tab is a link in the repository's top navigation bar. It has the accessible name "Settings". The Pages option in the left sidebar is a link under the "Code and automation" group heading. The branch and folder dropdowns are standard elements - navigate with arrow keys.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid navigation habit is to prove where you are before activating controls. Headings, landmarks, and the address bar are not trivia; they are your map.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If I am listening before the workshop starts, what should settle in my mind first?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Deploy from a branch is still useful because GitHub reads files directly from a branch. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: Best practice: Use /docs to isolate the published content from source files, especially for projects with build pipelines where the output lives in a specific folder.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid Git habit is to know which branch you are on, what changed, and what confirmation you expect before you run the next command.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does the learner do first, second, and then after that?

speaker: ALEX
text: Put GitHub Actions into plain language. Use a workflow (YAML file in.github/workflows/) to build your site before publishing. The useful version is: A basic workflow for this project would.

speaker: ALEX
text: The room should hear these as checkpoints. Your source is Markdown and you need a build step (e.g. this project's scripts/build-html.js). You are using a static site generator like Jekyll, Hugo, or Eleventy. You want to run accessibility tests in CI before publishing.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Check out the repository; Run node scripts/build-html.js; and Upload the html/ folder as the Pages artifact. Keep it that plain: know where you are, make the move, check the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now it sounds like a workflow instead of a wall of instructions.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is where confidence comes from: not from never getting lost, but from knowing how to recover.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Where do you want a learner to place their attention here?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. This project has a pre-built HTML mirror of all Markdown content in the html/ folder, generated by scripts/build-html.js. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: To publish this as a GitHub Pages site.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid pull request habit is to separate three questions: what changed, why it changed, and what still needs review. That keeps the conversation useful instead of noisy.

speaker: ALEX
text: Before the learner moves on. This part earns its place because because GitHub Pages only supports / (root) or /docs as folder sources, and this project's output is in html/, you have two options. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the safe way to learn from that example?

speaker: ALEX
text: Option A1 - Copy html/ contents to docs/: Then set Pages source to branch master, folder /docs.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Copy the html/ output into docs/ for GitHub Pages; cp -r html/ docs/; git add docs/; git commit -m "Publish HTML output to docs/ for GitHub Pages"; git push. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should happen before anyone copies and runs it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the learner-facing version. If the project does not already use docs/ for Markdown sources, you could rename the output folder. Put another way, this project uses docs/ for Markdown source files, so this would conflict.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Update the build script output path first, then; git mv html/ docs/; git commit -m "Rename html/ to docs/ for GitHub Pages compatibility"; git push. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What would you say to someone who is already bracing for this to be too much?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Option B: GitHub Actions workflow: this workflow triggers on every push to master, rebuilds the HTML, and deploys the html/ folder.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the kind of detail that keeps a screen reader user oriented.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The named thing - the heading, tab, field, branch, or button - is the handhold.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the moment where the page starts to make sense. Anchor this part on 5. Custom Domains. GitHub Pages supports custom domains (e.g. This is the part to say slowly: learning.community-access.org instead of community-access.github.io/Learning-Room). The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How would you walk the room through that step by step?

speaker: ALEX
text: Setting up a custom domain has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is in repository Settings → Pages, enter your domain in the Custom domain field and click Save. Step two is GitHub creates a CNAME file in the repository root containing your domain name. Step three is at your DNS provider, create the appropriate records. The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next point gives the learner a handle. Do not treat GitHub's current A record IPs for apex domains as decoration. DNS changes can take up to 48 hours to propagate. That is not trivia. GitHub Pages checks and verifies the domain automatically once DNS is configured.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the version that sounds like an instructor, not a manual.

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Domain verification is still useful because to prevent domain takeover attacks, GitHub recommends verifying your custom domain in your account or organization settings (Settings → Pages → Add a domain). For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: This prevents others from claiming your domain for their GitHub Pages if you temporarily remove it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Turn that into a path someone can follow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Put 6. HTTPS and Security into plain language. GitHub Pages enforces HTTPS automatically for github.io subdomains. The useful version is: Never store secrets, API keys, or private data in a GitHub Pages repository. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: After DNS propagates, check Enforce HTTPS in Pages settings; GitHub Pages uses Let's Encrypt to provision a certificate automatically; and Enforcing HTTPS redirects all HTTP traffic to HTTPS. The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learner is not behind if they stop there and check the page.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Pausing to verify is not a detour; it is how you keep control of the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 7. Accessibility Considerations for Published Sites. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Publishing a site does not automatically make it accessible. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: Consider the following for the published HTML output.

speaker: ALEX
text: That connects to another useful point. Learning Cards: Accessibility of Published Sites has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is what that changes in practice. Verify that published HTML pages include a skip-to-content link as the first focusable element -- press Tab once after the page loads to check. Confirm the page has a descriptive title element and a single H1 heading -- these are announced first when the page opens. Test that all internal navigation links work and land on the correct heading or section. Run an automated contrast checker (axe, WAVE) on published pages after any CSS changes to confirm text remains readable. Test the published site at 200% browser zoom to verify layouts do not break or hide content. If the site uses a custom theme, verify it works in both forced-colors mode (Windows High Contrast) and standard mode.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Navigation and landmarks. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Navigation and landmarks: The HTML generated by scripts/build-html.js converts Markdown headings and structure to HTML.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the details that keep the idea from floating away. A landmark wrapping the primary content. Logical heading hierarchy (H1 → H2 → H3). A page that reflects the document topic.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the practical turn. Here is the learner-facing version. For sites with repeated navigation on every page, add a skip link as the first element in. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Focus management for single-page navigation. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Focus management for single-page navigation: if the site uses JavaScript to load content without full page reloads, manage focus explicitly when content changes. That matters in practice: Move focus to the new or a wrapper with tabindex="-1" after navigation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a useful checkpoint before anyone starts pressing keys.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Checkpoints turn uncertainty into information.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the thread going. Anchor this part on Image alt text. All images in published pages should have appropriate alt attributes. This is the part to say slowly: Decorative images should use alt="" (empty, not missing) to be skipped by screen readers.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Color contrast. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: run the published pages through an automated checker (axe, WAVE) after changes to the stylesheet to verify contrast ratios remain compliant.

speaker: ALEX
text: Another way to ground it. Do not treat Testing the published site as decoration. After deployment, test the live URL rather than only local files. That is not trivia. mixed content, broken relative links, missing files) only appear when served from a web server. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Recommended post-deployment checks. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Recommended post-deployment checks has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Navigate all pages keyboard-only. Then: Run axe DevTools on the index page and at least one content page. Next: Verify no broken links with a link checker (e.g. W3C Link Checker). Last: Test with a screen reader announcement of the page title. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the part worth saying out loud. Put 8. GitHub Actions and Continuous Deployment into plain language. Using the Actions workflow described in Section 4 means every push to master automatically rebuilds and redeploys the site.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that because it gives people permission to slow down.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is the goal. We want the page to feel explorable, not fragile.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Adding automated accessibility checks to the workflow. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Extend the workflow to fail the build if accessibility violations are found. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: This uses the axe-core CLI to scan the built index page.

speaker: ALEX
text: Put that beside the next piece. Site not updating after a push has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Listen for the small confirmations in this list. Check the Actions tab for a failed deploy workflow. Check Settings → Pages - the most recent deployment timestamp should match your push. Hard-refresh the browser (Ctrl+F5 / Cmd+Shift+R) to bypass the cache. DNS TTL caching can delay custom domain updates up to the TTL value (often 1 hour).

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 404 on all pages except index. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: 404 on all pages except index: This usually indicates a base URL mismatch. The next useful detail is concrete: If your site is at https://user.github.io/my-repo/, all relative asset and link paths must account for the /my-repo/ path prefix.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now shift from knowing the term to using it. HTTPS certificate not provisioning has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the lesson becomes something you can check. Verify DNS records are correctly set. Ensure the domain is not proxied through a CDN (e.g. Cloudflare orange-cloud) - GitHub Pages needs to see the DNS record directly to provision the cert. Allow up to 24 hours after correct DNS propagation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Screen reader announces wrong page title. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Screen reader announces wrong page title: the published element is set during the HTML build step. That matters in practice: Update the template in scripts/build-html.js to ensure each page has a unique, descriptive title.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the part I would want someone to say out loud while they work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. A learner should always know what they are trying to prove before they take the next action.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should people carry with them after this?

speaker: ALEX
text: Carry the map. Know what page or tool you are in, know which action you are taking, and know what confirmation should follow. If the confirmation is missing, pause. That pause is not wasted time; it is professional judgment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a better way to say it than just follow the steps.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Steps matter, but understanding wins. That is episode 33. Next in the series is episode 34, where we keep building the same contributor muscles.
